title: Europe Struggles as Record-Breaking Heatwave Continues
subtitle: Governments and citizens face a difficult challenge as extreme temperatures test the limits of public services and infrastructure.

summary.en: Europe is currently experiencing its most severe and widespread heatwave on record, breaking temperature highs across multiple countries. The extreme weather has forced schools to close, disrupted transport, and caused significant strain on hospital systems. Experts warn that while past preparations have saved lives, the increasing intensity of these heat events is outpacing current safety measures. Scientists emphasize that human-caused climate change is making these dangerous conditions more frequent. Governments are now under pressure to improve long-term infrastructure to better protect vulnerable populations.
summary.zh: 歐洲目前正經歷有紀錄以來最嚴重且範圍最廣的熱浪，多國氣溫屢創新高。極端天氣迫使學校停課、交通中斷，並對醫療系統造成巨大壓力。專家警告，儘管過去的準備工作曾挽救生命，但這些高溫事件的強度正不斷增加，已超越現有的安全措施。科學家強調，人為造成的氣候變遷正使這些危險情況變得更加頻繁。各國政府目前面臨壓力，必須改善長期基礎設施，以更好地保護弱勢族群。

paragraphs.en: Europe is currently facing a record-breaking heatwave that has brought the continent to a standstill. As temperatures continue to reach historic highs, countries including France, the UK, and Switzerland have reported their hottest June days on record. This extreme weather has created dangerous conditions for millions of people, turning cities into ovens and forcing authorities to issue urgent warnings. The situation has become so severe that schools have asked parents to collect children early, and many workplaces have struggled to maintain normal operations.
paragraphs.zh: 歐洲目前正遭遇破紀錄的熱浪襲擊，導致整個大陸陷入停擺。隨著氣溫持續飆升至歷史新高，包括法國、英國與瑞士在內的多個國家，皆通報出現了有紀錄以來最炎熱的六月天。這種極端天氣為數百萬人帶來了危險，城市宛如烤箱，迫使當局發布緊急警報。情況嚴重到學校要求家長提早接回孩子，許多工作場所也難以維持正常運作。

paragraphs.en: According to The Guardian Health, this heatwave is an oppressive force made worse by carbon pollution. Pierre Masselot, an environmental epidemiologist at the London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine, noted that the current situation is reminiscent of the deadly heatwave that struck Europe in 2003. That event resulted in approximately 70,000 deaths, with older people and those living alone being the most affected. Masselot warned that while scientists have predicted these events for years, it is now painfully obvious that extreme heat is becoming the new normal for the continent.
paragraphs.zh: 根據《衛報健康》（The Guardian Health）報導，這波熱浪因碳污染而變得更加令人窒息。倫敦衛生與熱帶醫學院（London School of Hygiene & Tropical Medicine）的環境流行病學家馬瑟洛（Pierre Masselot）指出，當前的情況讓人想起二〇〇三年席捲歐洲的那場致命熱浪。當時該事件造成約七萬人死亡，其中長者與獨居者受創最深。馬瑟洛警告，儘管科學家多年來早已預測到這些事件，但現在顯而易見的是，極端高溫正成為歐洲大陸的新常態。

paragraphs.en: The impact of the heat is being felt across all parts of society. Hospitals in England have reported critical incidents, with some cooling systems failing and essential computer systems stalling. In France, the situation is equally difficult; more than 55 people have drowned while trying to cool off in water, and four young children have tragically died after being left in hot cars. Furthermore, the heat has affected energy production, as two nuclear reactors in France were forced to close because there was not enough cool water available to keep them safe.
paragraphs.zh: 高溫的影響已波及社會各個層面。英格蘭的醫院通報了多起重大事故，部分冷卻系統故障，關鍵的電腦系統也隨之停擺。法國的情況同樣嚴峻；有超過五十人在試圖下水消暑時溺斃，還有四名幼童不幸因被遺留在高溫車內而死亡。此外，熱浪也影響了能源生產，法國有兩座核子反應爐因缺乏足夠的冷卻水以維持安全運作，被迫關閉。

paragraphs.en: There is a debate about whether Europe has learned enough from past disasters. Following the 2003 heatwave, many governments introduced early warning systems—a set of alerts and plans to help people stay safe when temperatures rise. These plans include limiting travel, closing schools, and cancelling non-urgent medical appointments. Research suggests these efforts have been successful. A study from last November found that if the 2003 heatwave happened today, the death toll would be 75% lower because of these new safety measures.
paragraphs.zh: 關於歐洲是否已從過去的災難中汲取足夠教訓，目前仍存在爭論。在二〇〇三年熱浪過後，許多政府引進了早期預警系統，這是一套在氣溫升高時協助民眾保持安全的警報與計畫。這些措施包括限制交通、關閉學校以及取消非緊急的醫療預約。研究顯示這些努力已見成效。去年十一月的一項研究發現，若二〇〇三年那場熱浪發生在今日，由於這些新的安全措施，死亡人數將會減少百分之七十五。

paragraphs.en: However, experts argue that these adaptations may not be enough. The Guardian Health reports that while early warning systems are helpful, heatwaves are becoming hotter, longer, and more frequent. It is currently unclear if government efforts can keep up with the rising temperatures caused by planet-heating pollution. Many buildings, especially in France, still have poor protection against high heat, leaving residents vulnerable inside their own homes.
paragraphs.zh: 然而，專家認為這些調適措施可能還不夠。《衛報健康》報導指出，雖然早期預警系統有所幫助，但熱浪正變得更熱、更長且更頻繁。目前尚不清楚政府的努力是否能跟上由造成地球暖化的污染所導致的氣溫上升速度。許多建築物，特別是在法國，對高溫的防護能力依然不足，導致居民在自家屋內也暴露於風險之中。

paragraphs.en: Looking toward the future, the outlook remains concerning. Scientists warn that global heating will likely move past the 1.5C target—a goal set by world leaders to limit the rise in global temperatures—by the end of the century. For younger generations, these extreme weather events will be a regular part of life. As the continent continues to swelter, the challenge for leaders is to move beyond temporary emergency responses and invest in long-term changes to infrastructure, such as better building design and improved urban planning, to protect citizens from a warming world.
paragraphs.zh: 展望未來，前景依然令人擔憂。科學家警告，全球暖化極有可能在本世紀末超過攝氏一點五度的目標，這是世界各國領袖為限制全球氣溫升幅所設定的門檻。對於年輕世代而言，這些極端天氣事件將成為生活的一部分。隨著歐洲持續處於酷熱之中，各國領袖面臨的挑戰在於，必須超越臨時的緊急應變措施，轉而投資於基礎設施的長期變革，例如更好的建築設計與改善都市規劃，以保護公民免受暖化世界的威脅。

keyTerms.definitionEn: A situation in which all activity or movement has stopped.
keyTerms.noteZh: 此詞常與 bring 搭配使用，形容活動完全停止。文中：Europe is currently facing a record-breaking heatwave that has brought the continent to a standstill.

keyTerms.definitionEn: To stop making progress or to stop working properly.
keyTerms.definitionZh: （機器或系統）故障、停擺、無法運作。
keyTerms.noteZh: 常見於描述車輛引擎熄火，這裡指電腦系統運作停擺。文中：Hospitals in England have reported critical incidents, with some cooling systems failing and essential computer systems stalling.

keyTerms.definitionEn: To suffer because of the extreme heat.
keyTerms.noteZh: 此詞較少見，專指因高溫而感到極度不適。文中：As the continent continues to swelter, the challenge for leaders is to move beyond temporary emergency responses and invest in long-term changes to infrastructure, such as better building design and improved urban planning, to protect citizens from a warming world.

sources.name: The Guardian Health
sources.title: ‘A sad inevitability’: after decades of climate warnings, why is Europe so unprepared for rising heat?

previousArticles.title: Europe Faces Dangerous New Highs as Record Heatwave Continues
previousArticles.summaryEn: A severe heatwave is causing record-breaking temperatures across Europe, leading to dangerous conditions for millions. Countries like the UK, France, and Spain are seeing their highest temperatures in decades. Authorities have issued red alerts and taken emergency measures to protect public health. The extreme heat has also forced the shutdown of nuclear reactors and caused significant disruption to transport. Scientists warn that these events are becoming more frequent due to climate change.

previousArticles.title: Europe Struggles as Record-Breaking Heatwave Grips the Continent
previousArticles.summaryEn: A severe heatwave is causing record-breaking temperatures across Europe, leading to dangerous conditions for millions. Countries including the UK, France, and Germany have seen their highest temperatures in decades. Authorities have issued red alerts and taken emergency measures to protect public health. Economists warn that the extreme heat is threatening productivity and damaging economic growth. Experts suggest that Europe must adapt its infrastructure to handle these increasingly common weather events.

previousArticles.title: Europe Faces Dangerous New Highs as Heatwave Continues
previousArticles.summaryEn: A severe heatwave is currently affecting millions of people across Europe, leading to record-breaking temperatures in countries like the UK, France, and Spain. Experts warn that human-induced climate change is making these events more frequent and dangerous. Governments are responding with emergency plans to keep citizens safe. Schools, hospitals, and transport networks are struggling to handle the extreme heat. The World Health Organization has warned that these temperatures are putting lives at risk.

quiz.question: According to the article, what was a specific consequence of the heatwave on energy production in France?

options.A: Public transport systems were forced to shut down due to track warping.
options.B: Two nuclear reactors were shut down because of a lack of cooling water.
options.C: Energy prices spiked, causing widespread protests across major cities.
options.D: Power grids failed, leaving millions of people without electricity.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 B。文章第三段明確指出「the heat has affected energy production, as two nuclear reactors in France were forced to close because there was not enough cool water available to keep them safe」。選項 A 並未提及；選項 C 雖然提到能源，但文中未討論價格或抗議；選項 D 雖然提到醫院電腦系統故障，但未提及全國性的電網崩潰。

quiz.question: What can be inferred about the effectiveness of current government strategies against heatwaves?

options.A: They have completely solved the problem of heat-related fatalities.
options.B: They are perfectly adequate for the current climate but need to be updated for future warming.
options.C: They have reduced the death toll compared to 2003, but may struggle to keep pace with intensifying heat.
options.D: They are primarily focused on long-term urban planning rather than emergency responses.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 C。第四段提到新的預警系統讓死亡率降低了 75%，顯示有成效；然而第五段指出「experts argue that these adaptations may not be enough」且「it is currently unclear if government efforts can keep up」。選項 A 過於絕對；選項 B 錯誤，因為專家認為現有系統已不足以應對；選項 D 錯誤，因為文章最後一段提到政府目前多為「temporary emergency responses」，需要轉向長期的基礎設施投資。

quiz.question: In the second paragraph, what does the author mean by describing the heatwave as an 'oppressive force'?

options.A: A weather event that is difficult to bear and causes widespread suffering.
options.B: A political movement that restricts the freedom of European citizens.
options.C: A military strategy used by countries to control their borders.
options.D: A natural phenomenon that is physically impossible to measure or predict.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 A。在此上下文中，「oppressive」形容熱浪帶來的壓迫感，即極度高溫讓人難以忍受並造成痛苦。選項 B 將其誤解為政治壓迫；選項 C 與軍事策略無關；選項 D 錯誤，因為文中提到科學家一直在預測這些事件，並非無法測量。此詞彙在此處強調了熱浪對人類生活造成的沉重負擔。

quiz.question: What is the central message of the article regarding the current European heatwave?

options.A: The 2003 heatwave was a unique event that is unlikely to happen again.
options.B: Technological advancements in cooling systems are the only way to solve the climate crisis.
options.C: Europe must transition from short-term emergency measures to long-term infrastructure improvements to adapt to a warming climate.
options.D: The primary cause of the heatwave is the poor design of buildings in France and the UK.

quiz.explanationZh: 正確答案為 C。文章從描述熱浪現狀，過渡到過去的應對措施，最後在結論段落強調「the challenge for leaders is to move beyond temporary emergency responses and invest in long-term changes to infrastructure」。選項 A 與文意相反；選項 B 將冷卻系統視為唯一解法，過於狹隘；選項 D 只提到建築問題，這只是文章討論的一小部分，並非全文核心主旨。
